memories.content: Project 'Ripple Pond' (/Users/jerry/pool): a top-down Three.js water game. Began as a video-faithful interactive pool, then the user PIVOTED it to 'Hunt the Fish'. Core engine the user explicitly likes & wants kept: the GPU FDTD ripple simulation + floating physics. Herding levels + Free Play remain as optional side modes; drains/ducklings were retired.

Hunt-the-Fish design (one 'stress = depth' axis, no separate health bar by deliberate choice): fish hide at depth, invisible in calm water and REVEALED by rippling (which also scares them). Grab & fling the duck to BUMP fish shallower; bump lift scales with the duck's impact speed (hard throw slams up, drag barely nudges). Scared fish HOLD depth (bumps accumulate); calm fish REST & sink deep (recover) → must maintain pressure. Cornered fish (rippled everywhere) HIDE under a beach ball; bank-shot the ball to flush them. Anti-pin: each bump triggers a guaranteed escape-dart. Catch all to win.

User preferences/working style: values REAL physical simulation and NATIVE/emergent mechanics over bolted-on rules; wants design reasoning + tradeoffs explained, not just code. Iterates via continuous steering — give a concrete build + measurements, ask focused questions, tune to feel. Wants me to playtest the live app myself and verify with evidence. Tends to ask 'is it harder/easier/cooler' feel-questions only they can judge.

Tech & run: Three.js r0.184.0 via CDN importmap (jsDelivr); GPU FDTD wave sim with multi-source Mexican-hat ripples (src/sim.js); fish rendered to a full-pool render target revealed by the water shader's slope-gated 'clarity' (src/underwater.js, src/water.js uBaseClarity/uReveal); fish AI + DisturbanceMemory in src/fish.js. Run: cd /Users/jerry/pool && python3 -m http.server 8080. Needs WebGL2 + EXT_color_buffer_float. Verified 60fps at Retina DPR2 (2400x1626). Tuning knobs: difficulty in src/fish.js; reveal in src/water.js; duck feel (drag/restitution/throw) in src/duck.js + game.js.

Verification workflow with Playwright MCP: screenshots are NOT returned inline and `filename` outputs land in the project root (clean them up). To SEE a WebGL render: a tiny local server (/tmp/pool_server.py) serves the app AND accepts POST /shot; in-page, renderer.render then canvas.toDataURL JPEG and fetch('/shot?name=...') → file in /tmp/pool_shots → Read it. main.js exposes window.__pond = {sim,water,game,camera,renderer,scene,THREE} for in-page instrumentation, real PointerEvent dispatch, FPS probes, and bot-driving tests. After testing: rm -rf .playwright-mcp and stray *.jpeg/*.txt.

Housekeeping for this project: keep README.md (run instructions + physics/design writeup + sources) and project memory in sync with each pivot. Self-contained — all art is procedural (tiles, duck, fish, balls), no binary assets. ~14 src modules. Always confirm console is clean and re-verify 60fps at forced DPR 2 after changes.
